items.evidence_text:
Client: Hi. Honestly, post-divorce I just want to make sure I’m not missing anything big. I’m managing fine day-to-day, but I want a plan.
Advisor: That makes total sense. I’ll keep this pretty plain-English. Let’s start with income—what’s coming in over a year, roughly?
Client: It’s business income, and annually it’s 58550.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And is that pretty steady month to month, or does it swing?
Client: It’s monthly, mostly steady. Some months are better, but it averages out.

Client: It’s monthly, mostly steady. Some months are better, but it averages out.
Advisor: Okay. Next, on the spending side—about what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in?
Client: Around 2600 a month. That’s the number I track.
Advisor: Perfect. When you say that, does it include things like insurance premiums and property costs, or is it more just living expenses?
Client: It’s basically everything I pay monthly. I mean—most of it. The long-term care premium is in there too.

Client: Hi. Honestly, post-divorce I just want to make sure I’m not missing anything big. I’m managing fine day-to-day, but I want a plan.
Advisor: That makes total sense. I’ll keep this pretty plain-English. Let’s start with income—what’s coming in over a year, roughly?
Client: It’s business income, and annually it’s 58550.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And is that pretty steady month to month, or does it swing?
Client: It’s monthly, mostly steady. Some months are better, but it averages out.

Client: No mortgage. The house is paid off.
Advisor: Nice. Let’s shift to what you’ve built—your investable accounts. If you add up the accounts you’d consider investments or savings you can deploy, what’s the total you’re working with?
Client: Total investable assets are 220850.
Advisor: Okay—hang on, I’m just writing that down. And we can break that into buckets so it’s not one big blob. What’s in regular taxable savings or cash at the bank?
Client: At the bank, taxable, it’s 104650.

Client: Yes. Private markets on the retirement platform, value is 30200.
Advisor: Thanks. And then your home—separate from investable assets—what’s the current value you’re using for your primary residence?
Client: 129400. It’s on an advisor platform view, but it’s my primary residence.
Advisor: Perfect. And just to close the loop on liabilities: you said no loans outstanding and no monthly debt payments—still correct?
Client: Correct. No loans outstanding.

Client: Hi. Honestly, post-divorce I just want to make sure I’m not missing anything big. I’m managing fine day-to-day, but I want a plan.
Advisor: That makes total sense. I’ll keep this pretty plain-English. Let’s start with income—what’s coming in over a year, roughly?
Client: It’s business income, and annually it’s 58550.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And is that pretty steady month to month, or does it swing?
Client: It’s monthly, mostly steady. Some months are better, but it averages out.

Client: Hi. Honestly, post-divorce I just want to make sure I’m not missing anything big. I’m managing fine day-to-day, but I want a plan.
Advisor: That makes total sense. I’ll keep this pretty plain-English. Let’s start with income—what’s coming in over a year, roughly?
Client: It’s business income, and annually it’s 58550.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And is that pretty steady month to month, or does it swing?
Client: It’s monthly, mostly steady. Some months are better, but it averages out.

Advisor: Got it. Next one is taxes—what bracket do you typically think you’re landing in these days? Even a ballpark is fine.
Client: I think it’s around the low thirties—like 30-something percent.
Advisor: Okay—so when you say 30-something, is it specifically 32%, or am I off?
Client: It’s 32%.
Advisor: Perfect. And then risk—when markets swing, how do you want the portfolio to behave? Conservative, moderate, aggressive?

Client: No, that’s it—nothing outstanding.
Advisor: Okay, Patricia—thanks for walking through the accounts and the long-term care policy. I want to zoom out for two quick household-level items so I’m not making assumptions.
Advisor: First, just to confirm your marital status right now—are you currently divorced, married, single, what should I put down?
Client: Divorced.
Advisor: Got it. Next one is taxes—what bracket do you typically think you’re landing in these days? Even a ballpark is fine.

Client: Aggressive. I can handle volatility, I just don’t want to do anything reckless.
Advisor: That makes sense. We can build guardrails without neutering growth—sorry, without taking all the growth out of it.
Advisor: Last admin-ish thing: how are we working together—are you looking for an RIA (registered investment advisor) relationship, or more of a one-time plan?
Client: RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Great—thank you. Let me just tie a bow on what we covered today so I’m not missing anything major.

Advisor: And how long is it set to run—do you have an end date on it?
Client: Covered until 2033-02-20.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. So next time we can talk about what you want this money to do—growth versus stability—and how aggressive you want to be within some guardrails.
Advisor: Before we wrap, Patricia, I want to make sure the debt picture is captured cleanly. When you say “no loans,” what’s the total loans outstanding across everything—student loans, car, credit cards, anything?
Client: Total loans outstanding is 0.

Advisor: Perfect. And then non-mortgage debt—so credit cards you carry month to month, personal loans, car note—what’s that total outstanding?
Client: Non-mortgage debt outstanding is 0.
Advisor: Great. Just to sanity-check—sometimes people mean “no payments” but there’s still a balance floating. You’re saying balances are zero too, right?
Client: Right. No balances—0.
Advisor: Thanks. Switching gears for a second—on the property side, what value are you using for the home today?

Client: Total investable assets are 220850.
Advisor: Okay—hang on, I’m just writing that down. And we can break that into buckets so it’s not one big blob. What’s in regular taxable savings or cash at the bank?
Client: At the bank, taxable, it’s 104650.
Advisor: Got it. And retirement—do you have a 401(k) or IRA-type account?
Client: Yes, on a retirement platform—401k/IRA bucket is 57650.

Advisor: Not weird at all—sometimes the statements label things oddly. Any private investments—private markets, that kind of thing?
Client: Yes. Private markets on the retirement platform, value is 30200.
Advisor: Thanks. And then your home—separate from investable assets—what’s the current value you’re using for your primary residence?
Client: 129400. It’s on an advisor platform view, but it’s my primary residence.
Advisor: Perfect. And just to close the loop on liabilities: you said no loans outstanding and no monthly debt payments—still correct?

Advisor: First one: in the household, are you the primary decision-maker on the finances, or is anyone else involved?
Client: It’s just me—primary.
Advisor: Got it. And work-wise, are you currently employed, self-employed, between roles—what’s the status right now?
Client: Employed.
Advisor: Okay. When you say employed, is that like W-2 somewhere, or more you’re running the business but actively working in it? I’m not trying to split hairs—I just want the right picture.

Advisor: Okay. When you say employed, is that like W-2 somewhere, or more you’re running the business but actively working in it? I’m not trying to split hairs—I just want the right picture.
Client: I’m running it, but yes, I’m actively working—so, employed.
Advisor: Makes sense. And what would you call your general line of work—like sales, marketing, operations, tech—what bucket fits best?
Client: Sales.
Advisor: Perfect. And just to anchor the income number to you personally—your gross annual income is still 58550, right?

Client: Total investable assets are 220850.
Advisor: Okay—hang on, I’m just writing that down. And we can break that into buckets so it’s not one big blob. What’s in regular taxable savings or cash at the bank?

Client: Sounds good.
Advisor: Okay—before we hop off for real, Patricia, one tiny admin thing so my notes match how we label things in the plan.
Advisor: In our file, I have you marked as Client 1. Is that correct—you're Client 1?
Client: Yes, I’m Client 1.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you. I’ll leave it there.
